ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Singaporean steamed chicken rice; closest match is the generic chicken rice dish class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Singaporean steamed chicken rice; A0CKM (chicken and rice dish) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for this prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard hawker-centre serving of steamed chicken rice in Singapore typically comprises one plate of fragrant rice (approximately 200g) topped with sliced poached chicken (approximately 120–130g) and served with condiments, totalling approximately 350g.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Rice (Steamed) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Steamed Chicken Rice (cr_id F05100133), confidence 0.95. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken rice composite (USDA NDB 35188) as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (jasmine, cooked)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite Singaporean steamed chicken rice dish. GI of 68 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for jasmine white rice (cooked), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 56.6g carbohydrate in 350g serving) / 100 ≈ 38.5. Carbohydrate per serving = 23.43 × 3.5 = 82.0g; however, rice component (~200g) contributes approximately 56.6g available carbohydrate, used for GL calculation. Input record GI value of 68 and GL of 15.9 per 100g are consistent with this analogous estimate and are retained.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content of 407 mg per 100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold, and the dish is typically served with additional high-sodium condiments (soy sauce, chilli sauce, dark soy sauce) that substantially increase total sodium intake per meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce sodium load, request condiments on the side and use sparingly, and avoid adding extra soy or dark soy sauce; a standard 350g serving delivers approximately 1,425 mg sodium before condiments, representing over 60% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Jasmine white rice has a GI of approximately 68 (medium-high) and the dish provides 23.4g carbohydrate per 100g with only 0.91g dietary fibre per 100g, yielding an unfavourable carbohydrate-to-fibre ratio that promotes rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller rice portion (half-plate), supplement with non-starchy vegetables to lower the meal glycaemic load, avoid sweet dark soy sauce, and consider requesting brown rice if available; a full 350g serving delivers approximately 82g total carbohydrate, which is excessive for a single meal in glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is moderate at 4.2g/100g with an estimated saturated fat of approximately 1.1g/100g; however, the high sodium content (407 mg/100g) and typical accompaniment of fatty chicken skin and high-sodium sauces elevate cardiovascular risk, particularly for individuals with existing hypertension or dyslipidaemia.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should remove chicken skin before eating to reduce saturated fat intake, request condiments on the side to control sodium, limit portion size to one serving, and choose steamed over roasted chicken to minimise added fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined white rice contributes to high glycaemic load that may drive pro-inflammatory mediators; sodium at 407 mg/100g may exacerbate tissue oedema associated with RA flares.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request generous ginger paste (gingerols have anti-inflammatory properties), remove chicken skin to reduce saturated fat, limit soy-based sauces to reduce sodium, and consider brown rice substitution to lower glycaemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Chicken Rice (蒸鸡饭) is a quintessential Singaporean hawker dish of Chinese Hainanese origin, consisting of tender poached or lightly steamed chicken served over fragrant jasmine rice that has been cooked in seasoned chicken broth with ginger, garlic, and pandan leaves. The chicken is prepared by gentle poaching in a ginger-and-scallion broth until just cooked through, then chilled in ice water to achieve a silky, smooth skin and juicy, tender flesh. The rice is cooked by first toasting raw jasmine grains in rendered chicken fat before simmering in the poaching broth, resulting in a rich, aromatic, slightly oily rice with a distinct savoury flavour. The dish is traditionally served with a trio of dipping sauces: a ginger-sesame paste, a sweet dark soy sauce, and a fresh chilli-garlic sauce. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides 161 kcal, 7.45g protein, 4.2g fat, 23.4g carbohydrate, 0.91g dietary fibre, and 407mg sodium. The high sodium content (407 mg/100g) makes this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension, and the medium-to-high glycaemic index of the jasmine rice component (GI ~68) makes portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker centres and is a staple breakfast and working-lunch option across all ethnic communities in Singapore. It is commonly found at hawker centres, food courts, and coffee shops island-wide.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken rice Singapore?
Is steamed chicken rice good for diabetics?
Can I eat chicken rice with high blood pressure?
Steamed vs roasted chicken rice calories

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95, reviewed status), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The GI value of 68 is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database entry for jasmine white rice, the dominant carbohydrate component. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy as HPB does not report it separately, reducing overall confidence marginally but not below high given the strong primary source alignment.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.1g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for a comparable chicken-and-rice composite dish, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this record. This value should be updated when HPB publishes disaggregated fatty acid data. 2. The following fields are set to -1 (data not available in HPB or USDA SR Legacy for this composite dish as of 2026-04-16): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this composite Singaporean hawker dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of the processing date. 3. GI value of 68 is an analogous estimate based on jasmine white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for the composite steamed chicken rice dish has been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2026-04-16. 4. The input record listed the condition key as 'diabetes'; this has been corrected to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas v2 requirements. 5. Glycemic load per serving (38.5) is calculated for a standard 350g plate serving. The input record's GL of 15.9 represents GL per 100g, which is retained as the per-100g reference; the per-serving GL of 38.5 is the clinically relevant figure. 6. The rheumatoid arthritis caution in the input referencing nightshades (tomato, potato, eggplant, pepper, chilli) has been retained in the additional_conditions section; however, standard steamed chicken rice does not contain nightshade vegetables — this caution applies only to the chilli sauce condiment served alongside.
